name: fp-ts-errors
description: Handle errors as values using fp-ts Either and TaskEither for cleaner, more predictable TypeScript code. Use when implementing error handling patterns with fp-ts.
capabilities: Convert throwing functions to Either using tryCatch and tryCatchK
Chain multiple operations that might fail using chain and chainW
Collect multiple validation errors using validation applicative
Handle async operations with TaskEither and retry logic
Transform between Either, Option, Promise, and TaskEither patterns
Build type-safe API error handling with structured error types
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is educational documentation teaching fp-ts error handling patterns in TypeScript. The static analyzer produced 127 false positives by misidentifying Markdown code block backticks as shell commands. All network references are code examples demonstrating TaskEither usage, not executable code. No actual command execution, network calls, or system access exists in this skill.
valueStatement: Stop wrestling with try/catch spaghetti code. Learn to handle errors as type-safe values using fp-ts Either and TaskEither for predictable, composable error handling.
userTitle: Master fp-ts Error Handling in TypeScript
limitations: Requires fp-ts library installed in your TypeScript project
Learning curve for developers unfamiliar with functional programming concepts
Verbose syntax compared to simple try/catch for basic error cases
Not suitable for projects that cannot adopt functional programming patterns

useCases.targetUser: Frontend developers building registration forms and data entry interfaces
useCases.title: Form Validation with Error Accumulation
useCases.description: Validate user input forms and collect all validation errors at once instead of failing on the first error.

useCases.targetUser: Backend and full-stack developers creating resilient API clients
useCases.title: API Error Handling Pipeline
useCases.description: Build robust API layers that handle network errors, HTTP status codes, and JSON parsing failures with typed error results.

useCases.targetUser: Backend developers building transaction-heavy services with Prisma or similar ORMs
useCases.title: Database Operation Chains
useCases.description: Chain multiple database operations where any failure returns a structured error without throwing exceptions.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Either Pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me convert this function to use fp-ts Either instead of throwing errors: [paste function]. Show how to handle the error case with fold.
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginner learning to replace try/catch with Either

promptTemplates.title: Chain Multiple Operations
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to chain these three operations: getUser, validatePermissions, and createResource. Each can fail with different error types. Show me how to use chainW to combine them with fp-ts Either.

promptTemplates.title: Form Validation with All Errors
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a form validator using fp-ts that validates email, password, and age fields. Use the validation applicative to collect ALL errors at once, not just the first one.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building user-facing forms with comprehensive validation feedback

promptTemplates.title: Async Retry with TaskEither
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to wrap this fetch call with TaskEither and add retry logic with exponential backoff. Handle both network errors and non-200 HTTP responses with structured error types.
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced async error handling with resilience patterns

bestPractices: Wrap throwing code at the boundaries (external APIs, file I/O, JSON parsing) and return Either internally
Use descriptive error types (unions or interfaces) instead of plain strings for better type safety
Apply TaskEither for async operations to maintain lazy evaluation and avoid premature execution
antiPatterns: Calling fold too early in the chain - handle errors at the end, not after each step
Mixing Either with thrown exceptions - commit to one error handling strategy consistently
Using Left for expected control flow like 'not found' - reserve errors for actual exceptional cases

faq.question: When should I use Either versus TaskEither?
faq.answer: Use Either for synchronous operations that might fail. Use TaskEither for async operations (Promises) that might fail. TaskEither is lazy and returns a function that produces Promise<Either<E, A>>.

faq.question: How do I convert existing try/catch code to fp-ts?
faq.answer: Wrap the throwing code with E.tryCatch for sync code or TE.tryCatch for async code. Provide an onError function to transform caught exceptions into your error type.

faq.question: What is the difference between chain and chainW?
faq.answer: chain requires both Either types to have the same error type. chainW (wider) automatically unions different error types, useful when chaining operations with different error types.

faq.question: How do I get the value out of an Either?
faq.answer: Use fold to handle both cases explicitly, getOrElse to provide a default for Left, or unsafeGetRight only when you are certain it is Right. Avoid unsafeGetRight in production code.

faq.question: Can I use fp-ts with my existing Promise-based code?
faq.answer: Yes. Use TE.tryCatch to wrap Promises, and use toPromise or execute the TaskEither function to convert back to Promise when needed for external APIs.

faq.question: How do I collect multiple validation errors instead of failing fast?
faq.answer: Use sequenceS with getApplicativeValidation and a Semigroup for your error type. This combines all validations and accumulates errors using the Semigroup's concat operation.
